pending_evaluation.article:
**Building Data Pipelines That Actually Work in Production**

A data pipeline is the backbone of any modern data operation. It moves raw data from its source — a database, an API, a file system — through a series of transformations, and delivers clean, structured data to its final destination. That destination might be a dashboard, a machine learning model, or a data warehouse. The design choices you make early in the pipeline have a significant impact on performance, reliability, and cost.

One of the most important decisions is choosing between batch processing and streaming. In batch processing, data is collected over a period of time and processed all at once. A sales compensation team, for example, might run a batch job every night to calculate commissions based on the day's closed deals. This approach is simple and cost-effective for use cases where real-time data is not critical.

Streaming, on the other hand, processes data continuously as it arrives. This is essential when decisions depend on fresh information. Consider a fraud detection system at a bank. If transactions are only processed in nightly batches, fraudulent activity could go undetected for hours. Streaming pipelines using tools like Apache Kafka or Apache Flink make it possible to analyze each transaction the moment it occurs.

Another critical concept in pipeline design is idempotency. An idempotent pipeline produces the same result even if it runs multiple times on the same data. This matters because failures happen. A server crashes, a network times out, or a cloud service goes down unexpectedly. If your pipeline is not idempotent, re-running it after a failure could duplicate records or corrupt aggregated metrics. Well-designed pipelines handle this by using unique record identifiers and upsert operations rather than simple inserts.

Data quality checks are another layer that separates good pipelines from great ones. Rather than trusting that upstream data is always clean, professional teams embed validation rules directly into the pipeline. These rules might check for null values in required fields, flag statistical outliers, or verify that record counts fall within expected ranges. Tools like Great Expectations and dbt tests have made this practice much more accessible.

Finally, observability is increasingly recognized as a first-class concern in pipeline engineering. Teams need to know when a pipeline is slow, when it fails silently, or when data volume drops suddenly. Building in logging, alerting, and data lineage tracking from the start saves enormous amounts of debugging time later.

The difference between a pipeline built quickly and one built well often becomes visible only under pressure — when data volumes spike, when a source system changes its schema, or when a business team asks why last month's numbers changed after a reprocessing job. Thinking through these scenarios at design time is what separates a fragile prototype from a production-grade system.
pending_evaluation.writing_task:
**Your Task:**

The article explains the concept of **idempotency** and why it is important in data pipeline design. In **2–3 sentences**, describe this concept in your own words. Your answer should explain:

- What idempotency means in a pipeline context
- Why it matters when something goes wrong

**Use this sentence starter if you need help beginning:**
*"Idempotency means that a pipeline..."*

Try to avoid copying sentences directly from the article. Use your own words as much as possible.
